enhancements: On the failures card, parse-empty moved to the right-hand column with the other informational counts (it is a valid outcome, not a failure), and the divider above it is gone.
The tokens-used card gained a Per Study column (tokens on that line over studies successfully processed) and column headings - Total, Per Study, Cost - right-aligned above their numbers.
Theme options (Light / Dark / Auto) now sit directly in the account menu instead of a sub-menu that could mis-align.
The resolution-rate trend on the run card now prints each run's rate under its point on the line.
Studies remaining now shows a percentage alongside the count.
The eligibility-rows card shows rows per study (total rows over studies successfully processed).
The failures card gained a second column - unresolved concepts, studies without embeddings and superseded results - the same maintenance counts as the Tools tab.
The Runs page is paginated: the newest 16 runs with controls for older pages, and the title shows the range and total (e.g. Runs 1-16 of 20,000).
The nav rail and footer now stay fixed while only the page content scrolls.
Dashboard header now reads Clinical Eligibility Encoding, with Pipeline overview as the subtitle.
The live activity feed is a fixed-height rolling ticker showing the newest 16 events, instead of growing as a run streams messages in.
The most-recent-run card now shows the loading shimmer while it refreshes, matching the other cards.

Start and estimated-finish times on the run card are shown in your local time zone in a single dd-MMM-yyyy HH:mm format, and time-per-study is now reported to a tenth of a second - useful as the average drops to a few seconds.
The rows trend on the eligibility-rows card is now rows per 100 trials rather than a raw per-run total, so runs of different sizes are comparable.
Token costs are labelled At equivalent foundation model prices, since inference runs on self-hosted hardware and the real cost is electricity.
Footer now reads Ken Hayward.

The most-recent-run card now refreshes with the rest of the dashboard - on the Reload button and when a run or job finishes - instead of staying frozen until a full page reload. Its progress ring, rows persisted, resolution rate, status and estimated finish all update in place.
The refresh button moved to the top-left, ahead of the Pipeline overview title, and is now filled in the same blue as the progress dial so it is easy to find.
The live activity feed is wider, taking room from the failures, tokens and most-recent-run cards; the studies-successful and rows-persisted tiles keep their size.
Hovering a left-rail icon now shows a short description of that tab, not just its name.
The Runs, History, Analysis and Results pages use the same slim side margin as the dashboard, and each now has a title and a one-line description of what it shows. Tools keeps its centered layout.

The dashboard now shows two small trend lines from the last seven runs: resolution rate on the most-recent-run card, with a plus/minus change against the run before it, and rows-per-run on the rows tile. These are per-run figures, labelled as such, and sit alongside the corpus-wide totals rather than replacing them. They appear once there are at least two runs to compare - a single run is not a trend - and need no charting library.

The dashboard figures now load in place and update themselves. The metric cards show a brief loading shimmer while the numbers are read, then fill in - so the page appears at once instead of waiting for the slowest query. When a run or a maintenance job finishes, the affected figures refresh on their own rather than waiting for you to reload. A Reload button re-reads everything on demand, bypassing the short-lived cache so it always reflects the current state. If the read fails - most often because your sign-in has expired - the dashboard now says so in plain language and stays usable, instead of showing a raw error or sitting under the shimmer forever.

The dashboard is redesigned around the numbers you actually watch. The most recent run now shows a progress ring and its key figures at a glance, UMLS resolution is a gauge, and failures are a donut you can read at a distance - every slice links straight through to that status in History. Studies successful, rows persisted and token use sit along the bottom, and the live activity feed moves to a column of its own that scrolls on its own rather than pushing the page around. On a 1920x1080 screen the whole thing fits without scrolling; on smaller windows it scrolls normally rather than hiding anything.

Parse-empty and the embeddings backlog are shown next to the failures rather than counted as failures. Parse-empty is a valid outcome - the model answered and there were no criteria to extract - and it is common enough that counting it as a failure would have made two thirds of the failure chart something that never broke. Interrupted trials stay amber rather than red, matching History, because the trial did not fail: the host stopped before it reached an outcome.
If you do not have permission to run the pipeline you now see a Read-only marker instead of an empty toolbar.

Groundwork for the dashboard redesign: the dashboard's corpus figures are now also available as data at /Home/Metrics, which is what will let the page show its numbers as they arrive, refresh itself when a run finishes, and give the Reload button something to do other than reload the whole page. Nothing on screen changes yet. The figures are the same ones the dashboard already shows and need the same sign-in to see.

Navigation moved from a bar across the top to a slim icon rail down the left side, on every page. Each tab keeps its name for screen readers and on hover, and the tab you are on is a filled tile rather than an underline. This buys back vertical space for the pages that need it. Your account menu - About, Manage Accounts, Audit Trail, Runtime Parameters, the database seed and the Light/Dark/Auto theme picker - is unchanged and now sits on the avatar at the foot of the rail, opening to the right.
fixes: The footer no longer relies on being positioned absolutely over a reserved strip of the page; it is part of the page layout, spans the full width beneath the rail, and its divider follows the light/dark theme instead of being a fixed light grey that all but vanished in dark mode.

enhancements: SQL logging is now usable instead of a firehose. Set Postgres__SlowCommandLogThresholdMs=50 and only commands taking at least 50ms are logged - everything faster vanishes, so an outlier is visible instead of buried under thousands of fast queries. Surviving commands print on ONE line with their duration and query, rather than spanning as many lines as the query has newlines. Set Logging__Console__FormatterName=simple and Logging__Console__FormatterOptions__SingleLine=true alongside it to get the whole entry on a single line: info: Npgsql.Command[2001] Command execution completed (duration=90ms): SELECT ... The default of 0 logs every command, so logs are unchanged unless you set a threshold. Query text is truncated at 500 characters, and parameter values are still never logged. The Debug-level start-of-command line always shows regardless of the threshold, because a query that hangs never finishes and so never reports a duration - that line is the only trace it leaves.

You can now see the database queries and language-model calls in the logs when diagnosing something. Setting Logging__LogLevel__Default=Debug turns on all of it; Logging__LogLevel__Npgsql=Information shows every SQL command with its duration, and Logging__LogLevel__System.Net.Http.HttpClient=Information shows each call to the model server with its URL, status and timing. Everything stays off by default and the logs are unchanged unless you ask. Note this is a firehose during a real run - thousands of commands and one line per model call - so it is for diagnosis rather than leaving on. Query parameters are deliberately never logged, because they carry criteria text, model responses and account details.
fixes: Log levels for the database, model-server and retry categories were impossible to change. They were pinned to Warning in code as well as in configuration, and a code-level pin silently overrides configuration - so no setting could turn them back on, and nothing reported that. On top of that the database connections were never given a logger at all, so there was no database logging to enable in the first place. The pins are now defaults that any explicit setting overrides, and the connections carry the logger. With nothing configured the logs are exactly as quiet as before, from any launch directory.

enhancements: Tools: a new 'Exact trials remaining' card calculates the real backlog on demand, for when you are close to draining the queue. It applies the same filter the pipeline uses when choosing trials, so it excludes trials that will never be processed. It takes around 26 seconds against the hosted AACT database, which is why it runs only when you press the button rather than on every page view.
fixes: The dashboard loads in well under a second again, instead of taking around ten. Its 'trials remaining' figure was counting only the trials the pipeline would actually select, which sounds right but means reading the eligibility text of every trial in AACT - about 26 seconds over the internet, paid by whoever loaded the page after the figure went stale. It now counts all trials in AACT, which the database answers in about a fifth of a second. The trade is that the number is now roughly 0.3% high and settles near 1,700 rather than reaching zero, since it includes trials that can never be processed. The card was already marked approximate, and the new Tools card gives the exact number whenever it matters.

enhancements: The extraction pipeline can now use more than one GPU, including ones that are only available some of the time. A load balancer (HAProxy) runs as part of the stack and sits in front of the language-model servers; the pipeline talks to it instead of to a single machine. Each server is declared with its address and how many requests it can handle at once, and the balancer sends it no more than that, queueing the rest - so an occasional second or third GPU roughly multiplies throughput while it is switched on, and nothing needs restarting when one appears or disappears. Servers are checked continuously and taken out of rotation before any work is sent to them. A status page at port 8404 shows which are up and how busy each is. Configure the servers with LLM_GPU_* in .env; leave the extra ones out if you only have one. Every server must run the same model with the same settings, since which one answers a given trial is arbitrary. The normalization and embedding endpoints are unchanged and still address their servers directly.

fixes: Settings given on the command line are no longer silently ignored. The .env file is loaded into the environment at start-up, and it was overwriting values that had already been set - so running something like `Postgres__ConnectionStringOutput=... dotnet run -- migrate` from inside the repository used whatever .env said instead of what was asked for, with no error and no log line to say so. Since a developer .env usually points at the production database, a command aimed at a test database could quietly reach production. An already-set value now wins and .env fills in only what is missing. Nothing else changes: with nothing set, .env still supplies every value as before, and containers have no .env at all.

A batch started from the command line can no longer run at the same time as one started from the dashboard. The dashboard already refused to start a second batch while one was in flight, but that check only applied within the dashboard's own process - it could not see `elig run`, which drives the same pipeline against the same database. Both would independently work out which trials to process, pick overlapping ones, and compete for the same model slots: wasted time on the one resource that actually limits throughput. Whichever starts first now takes a lock in the database, and the other is refused - the dashboard reports the run as cancelled with the reason in the log, the command line prints a message and exits without starting. The lock is released when the run finishes, and automatically if the process is killed, so it can never get stuck.

Trials cut short by the host stopping are no longer lost without trace. A study row is marked 'running' the moment a trial starts and updated when it finishes, so a host killed mid-trial left the row marked 'running' for ever: the dashboard counted it as neither a success nor a failure, and the pipeline treated the trial as already attempted, so no later batch ever picked it up. On start-up the dashboard now records any study still marked 'running' and older than six hours as 'interrupted' - a new status that appears in the Studies failed card and in History, where the trial can be re-run like any other. The six-hour limit is deliberate and configurable via Postgres:InterruptedStudyThresholdHours (0 disables it): a single trial can legitimately take around two hours, and a batch started from the command line can be running against the same database at the same time, so nothing younger than the limit is touched. A trial that turns out to have still been running is corrected back to its real outcome when it finishes. Studies already stranded are recovered the next time the dashboard starts.

enhancements: Tools: a new 'Remove superseded results' card shows how many study attempts have been replaced by a later run - counting every superseded attempt, successful or failed - and lets you delete them, keeping the most recent attempt for each trial. It is a plain database delete rather than a background job, so there is no progress bar; it asks for confirmation first, naming the number of rows, because it cannot be undone. The extracted criteria in public.eligibility are not touched, and no trial becomes eligible for reprocessing: one attempt per trial always remains, so the pipeline's record of what it has already done is unchanged. The count matches exactly what the Studies tab hides under 'Hide superseded attempts'. Requires pipeline-operator permission, is blocked while a run or another tool is in flight, and is recorded in the audit trail.

fixes: Deploy: the deploy scripts now put .env where docker compose actually reads it. Both deploy.ps1 and deploy-windows.ps1 expected .env at the repository root and copied it there on the target, but the compose file gives each service `env_file: - .env`, and Compose resolves that relative to the compose file - so the containers needed it at deploy/eligibility-pipeline/.env and a deploy onto a clean target failed with 'env file ...deploy/eligibility-pipeline/.env not found'. The scripts now default -EnvFile to deploy/eligibility-pipeline/.env and transfer it to the same path on the target. Passing -EnvFile explicitly still works and now ships whichever file you name to the correct destination. A repository-root .env is unaffected and is still what `dotnet run` reads for local development.

enhancements: Dashboard: the Studies successful card now also shows roughly how many trials are left to process, so you can see the size of the remaining backlog at a glance. The figure is approximate - it is the number of AACT trials that pass the selection filter minus the number already attempted, rather than the exact anti-join the batch selector runs, which is far too slow for a page load. It is omitted entirely when there is no AACT source configured (for example the seeded quickstart), rather than showing a misleading zero.

Runtime Parameters: the panel now shows which Postgres the source (ctgov) and output databases actually point at, so you can tell a local AACT copy from a remote one without digging through the environment. Only host, port, and database name are shown - the credentials in the connection string are never displayed.
fixes: Dashboard: the two batch buttons are now named 'Trigger Earliest' and 'Trigger Latest', matching the 'Earliest unprocessed' and 'Most recent unprocessed' fields above them. The previous 'Trigger batch' / 'Trigger recent' names did not make the pairing obvious.

Deploy: `docker compose build` now works from deploy/eligibility-pipeline. The compose file pointed `dockerfile:` at a bare `Dockerfile.web` / `Dockerfile.cli` while setting `context:` to the repository root, and Docker resolves the dockerfile path relative to the context rather than to the compose file - so the build failed with 'failed to read dockerfile: open Dockerfile.web: no such file or directory'. Both services now name the dockerfile relative to the context. The context itself is unchanged and must stay at the repository root, since the Dockerfiles copy the whole tree and restore contexts/eligibility/Eligibility.sln.

enhancements: Dashboard and Results pages load faster. Both were recomputing whole-corpus aggregates from scratch on every single page view - the dashboard's headline metrics (about 700 ms) and the Results page's filter dropdown lists (about 1150 ms, on every pager click and filter change too). Those figures are aggregates over the entire corpus, so they only change when a run persists new trials; they are now cached in memory for 60 seconds, which takes roughly 1.8 seconds of database work off the two busiest pages. Set Web:CorpusCacheTtlSeconds to 0 to disable the cache and always read live.

Results filter dropdowns no longer do work they throw away. Building the dropdown lists ran a SELECT DISTINCT over all 3.9M eligibility rows for each of six columns, then discarded any column whose value count exceeded the dropdown threshold - so about 950 ms of every Results page view was spent computing lists that were immediately dropped. The query now asks the database planner for its distinct-count estimate first and skips the scan entirely for columns that are provably over the threshold, cutting the six-column total from about 1150 ms to about 240 ms. Columns near the threshold, and any column the planner has no estimate for, are still counted exactly, so the dropdowns themselves are unchanged.

fixes: Quickstart: the web service's output database connection (Postgres__ConnectionStringOutput) can now be overridden from deploy/quickstart/.env, matching how the AACT source already works. Previously it was hard-coded in the compose file's environment block, which silently overrode any .env value - so pointing the dashboard at a different database (for example, to export/import a production instance's embeddings) did not take effect. The embedded default is unchanged and still tracks the POSTGRES_* variables, so zero-config `docker compose up` is unaffected.

enhancements: Embeddings export and import (Owners): share the corpus similarity index that powers the Authoring Analysis tab's Find Similar. The owner 'Database seed & embeddings' dialog now has an Embeddings tab that exports the index to a downloadable archive (named with the embedding model, since similarity is only comparable within one model) and imports one back - either from a file or a release-asset URL. Importing clears the existing index first, then loads the archive, so others can pick up your published embeddings without re-running embed-studies. The dialog shows the current index (row count and model) and reports what was exported or imported.

Authoring is now a dashboard tab. Instead of opening in a separate page, Authoring lives alongside Dashboard, Runs, and the other tabs. A study list sits in a panel on the left; click a study to open it in the editor beside the list. Collapse the panel with the chevron to give the editor the full page width - your choice is remembered between visits.
fixes: Authoring layout: restored the study-list and editor styling that was lost when the feature was first brought over, so the two-pane view renders correctly.

enhancements: Authoring (demo): a new 'Authoring' area lets you design a study that has not been registered yet and see how the processed data supports it. Start blank, from an existing trial, or from another authored study; fill in the study details and eligibility; then build the criteria list - either by hand or by mining the processed corpus. The Analysis tab finds similar trials, groups their common inclusion/exclusion criteria, and turns each group into a single clean criterion you can add, keeping a record of every source it came from. Export the finished criteria, or a full source-by-source audit, as CSV. Designing, editing, and exporting work with no extra setup; the corpus-mining Find Similar and Normalize steps need an embedding and language-model endpoint configured, and the similarity index built via Tools -> embed-studies.

Create database seed (Owners): generate a fresh, shareable database seed straight from the dashboard. The 'Create database seed' item on the owner account menu exports the six processed tables into a ready-to-load archive, shows live per-table progress as it runs, and offers the result as a download - so you can cut a new seed release as coverage grows and load it with the quick-start stack, no local database tools required.
fixes: Quick start: you can now run new inference from the quick-start stack against your own AACT database by setting the source connection string in .env; the bundled database stays the default, so browsing the seed and creating a seed keep working with no configuration.

Quickstart: the web service now reads deploy/quickstart/.env, so app settings placed there (e.g. Umls__Backend, Llm__*) actually reach the dashboard. Previously Compose used .env only for ${VAR} substitution inside the compose file and never injected it into the container, so those settings were silently ignored and the app fell back to its built-in defaults. The .env is loaded as an optional env_file, so the zero-config 'docker compose up' still works.

Hygiene: .gitignore now also excludes dotted dotenv variants (.env.local, .env.production, ...) that the previous *.env rule missed, while still keeping .env.example tracked - so an env file with real secrets cannot be committed by accident.

enhancements: Quick start with seed data: bring up the whole application - database, a large pre-inferenced dataset, and the dashboard - with a single 'docker compose up', and explore already-processed clinical-trial eligibility data immediately, without re-running the extraction pipeline. The dataset is downloaded and loaded automatically on first run, so there is nothing to set up.
fixes: Startup no longer logs a Postgres exception and stack trace when the co-located source database has no ctgov (AACT) schema - the expected case when running against the seeded standalone database. The source trial-selection performance index is now skipped with a clean informational message instead.

Tests: updated the dashboard version-surface tests to assert the current version. They had been left pinned to 0.1.1 after the 0.1.2 bump, so the integration suite failed on the version endpoint, footer, and AppVersion checks.

Deployment: the web and CLI containers can now reach a Postgres, LM Studio, or embedding server running on the Docker host. Each compose service maps host.docker.internal to the host gateway (required on Linux Docker Engine) and disables container IPv6 so the host name resolves over IPv4 only - this avoids 'Connection refused' / 'Network unreachable' errors when host services are addressed as host.docker.internal in .env.

enhancements: The platform logo now appears in the dashboard header, the About box, and the Release Notes page.

This is the first released version of the Eligibility Pipeline. Semantic versioning (Major.Minor.Build with a release date) starts here at 0.1.0 - there are no earlier releases, so this changelog begins now.

Version history: the pipeline is a .NET re-implementation of the production n8n eligibility-extraction workflow - trial selection, LLM extraction, UMLS concept resolution, and per-trial persistence - fronted by a SignalR dashboard and a CLI, with authentication, role-based authorization and auditing, and the corpus similarity index that feeds the Eligibility Authoring module. All of that prior work predates formal versioning and is rolled up into this 0.1.0 baseline.
Added: an About box that shows the current version and release date, and a Release Notes page that lists each version's enhancements and bug fixes.
